prompt: An unresponsive adult has gurgling respirations and vomitus in the mouth. Your first airway action is to:

options.text: Log-roll, suction the oropharynx, then reassess breathing
Insert an oropharyngeal airway without suction
Begin chest compressions immediately without airway care
Place the patient supine with head hyperextended only

explanation: Airway obstruction from secretions requires positioning and suction before adjuncts or ventilation.

distractorExplanations.b: Adjuncts do not clear material; suction first.
distractorExplanations.c: If breathing is present after clearing, compressions are not indicated.
distractorExplanations.d: Hyperextension alone does not clear vomitus.

prompt: Which patient is the best candidate for a nasopharyngeal (NPA) airway?

options.text: Apneic patient requiring bag-mask ventilation only
Semiconscious patient with intact gag reflex who needs basic airway support
Patient with suspected basilar skull fracture
Fully conscious patient refusing all care

explanation: NPA is tolerated in semiconscious patients with gag reflex; OPA may trigger gagging/vomiting.

distractorExplanations.a: BVM may be used without NPA; NPA helps maintain patency in some semiconscious patients.
distractorExplanations.c: NPA is contraindicated with basilar skull fracture risk.

prompt: Proper head-tilt chin-lift is contraindicated when you suspect:

options.text: Asthma exacerbation
Hypoglycemia
Cervical spine injury without manual inline stabilization
Heat exhaustion

explanation: Suspected spinal injury requires jaw-thrust/manual inline stabilization instead of head-tilt.

distractorExplanations.a: Airway opening may still be needed with spinal precautions.
distractorExplanations.b: Not a contraindication to airway maneuvers.

prompt: A conscious adult is choking, cannot speak, and has ineffective cough. You should:

options.text: Start rescue breathing without attempting dislodgment
Slap the back while the patient is sitting upright only
Wait for advanced providers before any intervention
Give abdominal thrusts (Heimlich) until obstruction clears or patient becomes unresponsive

explanation: Complete airway obstruction in a conscious adult warrants abdominal thrusts per AHA BLS.

distractorExplanations.a: Breaths cannot pass a complete obstruction.
distractorExplanations.b: Back blows may be used in cycles but thrusts are standard for adults.

prompt: When ventilating an apneic adult with a bag-valve-mask (BVM), adequate ventilation is indicated by:

options.text: Visible chest rise over about 1 second per breath at 10–12 breaths/min
Gastric distention only
Rapid forceful breaths at 30/min regardless of chest rise
Ventilating without a seal because leaks are acceptable

explanation: Each breath should produce visible chest rise without excessive rate or force.

distractorExplanations.b: Gastric distention suggests excessive volume or poor seal.
distractorExplanations.d: A proper mask seal is essential.

prompt: An oropharyngeal airway (OPA) is sized by measuring from:

options.text: Earlobe to xiphoid process
Corner of the mouth to the angle of the mandible
Nose to umbilicus
Shoulder to wrist

explanation: Standard OPA sizing uses mouth corner to mandibular angle.

distractorExplanations.a: That measurement is for NPA (nostril to tragus).

prompt: You are ventilating a patient with a BVM and resistance is high with poor chest rise. You should first:

options.text: Increase ventilatory rate to 30/min
Remove the BVM and wait
Reposition the head/jaw and confirm a mask seal
Administer high-flow oxygen without ventilation

explanation: Poor rise often reflects inadequate seal or airway positioning before increasing force/rate.

distractorExplanations.a: Higher rate worsens air trapping and gastric inflation.
distractorExplanations.d: Oxygen alone does not ventilate apneic patients.

prompt: Which finding suggests partial upper airway obstruction?

options.text: Silent chest with no air movement
Normal conversational speech
Regular painless breathing at 16/min
Stridor with hoarse voice and anxiety

explanation: Stridor and voice changes suggest partial obstruction of the upper airway.

distractorExplanations.a: Complete obstruction may be silent.

prompt: For a trauma patient requiring spinal precautions, opening the airway is best done with:

options.text: Jaw-thrust while maintaining manual inline stabilization
Head-tilt chin-lift alone
Hyperextension of the neck without stabilization
Nasotracheal intubation by the EMR

explanation: Jaw-thrust minimizes cervical motion when spinal injury is suspected.

distractorExplanations.d: EMRs do not perform advanced airway placement.

prompt: A patient with COPD receiving emergency oxygen via nonrebreather at 15 L/min becomes sleepy with shallow respirations. The EMR should:

options.text: Continue 15 L/min because more oxygen is always better
Reassess ventilatory effort and follow local protocol for titrated oxygen in COPD
Remove all oxygen permanently
Place an OPA in a fully conscious patient without indication

explanation: COPD patients may depend on hypoxic drive; titrate per protocol and monitor ventilatory status.

distractorExplanations.a: Uncontrolled high-flow O2 can suppress drive in some COPD patients.
distractorExplanations.c: Hypoxemia may still need controlled oxygen.
distractorExplanations.d: OPA is not indicated in fully conscious patients.

prompt: Suctioning the oropharynx should generally be limited to:

options.text: Continuous suction until the catheter melts
60 seconds without interruption
10 seconds with reoxygenation between attempts
Never suction trauma patients

explanation: Brief suction passes reduce hypoxia risk; reoxygenate between attempts.

distractorExplanations.a: Prolonged suction causes hypoxia.
distractorExplanations.d: Suction may be needed in trauma with secretions/vomitus.

prompt: Which oxygen delivery device provides the highest FiO2 when fitted properly?

options.text: Nasal cannula at 2 L/min
Simple face mask at 6 L/min
Venturi mask set to 24%
Nonrebreather mask with reservoir at adequate flow

explanation: A nonrebreather with reservoir and sufficient flow can deliver high FiO2.

distractorExplanations.a: Cannula delivers lower FiO2.
distractorExplanations.b: Simple mask is lower than nonrebreather.

prompt: An apneic adult patient after successful ROSC from CPR should receive:

options.text: Oxygen and ventilatory support per protocol with pulse oximetry monitoring
No oxygen because ROSC means normal oxygenation
Only oral glucose
Immediate discharge from scene

explanation: Post-ROSC patients often need oxygen and ventilatory support with close monitoring.

distractorExplanations.d: Transport and continued care are required.

prompt: When using a pocket mask with a one-way valve, the EMR should:

options.text: Blow as hard and fast as possible
Deliver breaths over 1 second watching for chest rise
Skip the valve to improve flow
Use only nasal prongs attached to the mask

explanation: Pocket mask ventilations follow BLS guidelines: slow breaths with visible rise.

distractorExplanations.a: Forceful rapid breaths increase gastric inflation.
distractorExplanations.d: Pocket masks are for mouth-to-mask ventilation.

prompt: Signs of worsening upper airway swelling (e.g., anaphylaxis) include:

options.text: Improving voice volume and disappearing wheeze
Stable oxygen saturation without exam changes
Progressive stridor, drooling, and difficulty swallowing
Only abdominal pain without respiratory signs

explanation: Stridor, drooling, and dysphagia suggest impending complete airway obstruction.

distractorExplanations.a: Improvement would not include stridor progression.
distractorExplanations.b: Exam may worsen before saturation drops.

prompt: Direct pressure fails to control severe extremity bleeding. Your next step is:

options.text: Remove the tourniquet and apply a bandage
Elevate the leg and wait 30 minutes
Apply ice directly inside the wound
Apply a tourniquet proximal to the wound and note the time applied

explanation: Life-threatening extremity hemorrhage uncontrolled by pressure warrants tourniquet application with time documented.

distractorExplanations.a: Do not remove an effective tourniquet in the field without medical direction.
distractorExplanations.b: Elevation alone may be insufficient for arterial bleeding.
distractorExplanations.c: Ice does not replace hemorrhage control.

prompt: A helmeted motorcycle rider is awake with stable vitals after a crash. Helmet removal should occur when:

options.text: Airway compromise, vomiting, or inadequate breathing cannot be managed with helmet in place
Never until hospital arrival regardless of airway or breathing
Immediately for all helmeted patients on arrival
Only if the patient requests cosmetic adjustment

explanation: Helmets remain if airway is manageable; remove when airway/breathing needs require it with spinal precautions.

distractorExplanations.c: Routine immediate removal increases motion if not indicated.

prompt: Which patient should be immobilized on a long backboard per typical EMR protocols?

options.text: Stable alert patient who walked away without complaint
Mechanism suggesting spinal injury with midline tenderness or neurologic deficit
Any patient with a sprained finger
Patient refusing all care but fully neurologically intact without tenderness

explanation: Spinal motion restriction is indicated with concerning mechanism plus exam findings.

distractorExplanations.a: Low suspicion with normal exam may not require immobilization per local protocol.
distractorExplanations.c: Finger sprain alone does not indicate spinal injury.

prompt: Signs of shock from hemorrhage include:

options.text: Warm dry skin with bradycardia only
Hypertension with bounding pulses only
Pale cool clammy skin, tachycardia, and delayed capillary refill
Normal mental status with no vital sign changes

explanation: Hypovolemic shock often presents with tachycardia, poor perfusion, and altered mental status.

distractorExplanations.a: Shock typically causes cool skin and tachycardia.
distractorExplanations.b: Late shock may drop pressure; bounding pulses are not typical early.
distractorExplanations.d: Shock often alters vitals and mentation.

prompt: An open fracture of the forearm with active bleeding should be managed by:

options.text: Pushing bone back into the wound before bandaging
Tourniquet applied over the fracture site distal to injury
Heat packs over the wound
Covering with sterile dressing, controlling bleeding, and splinting in position found

explanation: Do not replace exposed bone; cover, control hemorrhage, immobilize, and transport.

distractorExplanations.a: Replacing bone increases infection and tissue damage.
distractorExplanations.b: Tourniquet goes proximal to wound, not over it routinely.

prompt: When splinting a painful swollen ankle, the EMR should:

options.text: Splint in the position of comfort after neurovascular check
Force the foot into anatomical neutral despite severe pain
Remove the shoe and leave the joint unsupported
Apply heat before assessment

explanation: Splinting in position found after CMS check reduces movement and pain.

distractorExplanations.c: Immobilization is indicated for suspected fracture/sprain.
distractorExplanations.d: Heat is not initial care for acute trauma.

prompt: A patient impaled by a metal rod through the abdomen should receive:

options.text: Immediate removal of the object to bandage the hole
Stabilization of the object in place and rapid transport
Cutting the object flush without stabilization
Oral fluids to prevent dehydration only

explanation: Impaled objects remain in place unless they block airway management per medical direction.

distractorExplanations.a: Removal can cause uncontrolled hemorrhage.
distractorExplanations.c: Stabilize before cutting if shortening is needed for transport.
distractorExplanations.d: NPO status is typical for abdominal impalement.

prompt: Which burn should receive priority cooling and transport consideration?

options.text: Small superficial burn on the palm without respiratory symptoms
Minor sunburn on the back
Partial-thickness burn to circumferential chest with singed nasal hairs
Healed old burn scar itching

explanation: Circumferential chest burns and inhalation signs suggest airway/ventilation risk.

distractorExplanations.a: Still needs care but lower priority than inhalation injury signs.
distractorExplanations.d: Old scar is not acute burn.

prompt: Tension pneumothorax signs may include:

options.text: Bilateral equal breath sounds with normal BP
Only isolated toe pain
Bradycardia with hypertension only
Tracheal deviation away from the affected side, absent breath sounds, and shock

explanation: Tension pneumothorax causes unilateral absent sounds, JVD, hypotension, and possible tracheal shift.

distractorExplanations.a: Equal sounds argue against unilateral tension.

prompt: During manual inline stabilization, the EMR holding the head should:

options.text: Maintain alignment until collar and backboard immobilization are coordinated
Release briefly to apply a cervical collar without coordination
Hyperextend the neck to open airway without team communication
Allow the patient to sit up for comfort

explanation: Inline stabilization continues until an coordinated team applies collar/board.

distractorExplanations.c: Airway maneuvers use jaw thrust with stabilization.

prompt: A patient with an amputated finger should have the part:

options.text: Discarded at scene to prevent infection
Wrapped moist in sterile gauze, placed in waterproof bag on ice (not direct ice contact)
Submerged directly in ice water for hours
Heated to preserve tissue

explanation: Amputated parts are kept cool and moist without direct ice contact for possible reattachment.

distractorExplanations.a: Preserve when possible.

prompt: The primary purpose of the primary trauma survey (ABCDE) is to:

options.text: Complete full patient history before any interventions
Obtain insurance information first
Identify and treat immediate life threats in sequence
Splint all injuries before airway assessment

explanation: ABCDE prioritizes life threats: airway, breathing, circulation, disability, exposure.

distractorExplanations.a: History follows stabilization.

prompt: A flail chest segment may cause:

options.text: Improved lung expansion
Only cosmetic deformity without breathing impact
Bradycardia from improved oxygenation
Paradoxical chest wall movement and respiratory distress

explanation: Multiple rib fractures create unstable segment with paradoxical motion and hypoventilation.

distractorExplanations.a: Flail chest impairs ventilation.

prompt: When assessing pulse, motor, and sensation (CMS) distal to an injury, absent distal pulse after splinting means:

options.text: Loosen/adjust splint and reassess; prepare for rapid transport
Success; tighter splints improve pulses
Ignore because pain is expected
Remove all bandages and leave limb dangling

explanation: Loss of distal pulse after immobilization requires adjustment and urgent transport.

prompt: Scene size-up for trauma should include:

options.text: Only patient name and address
BSI, scene safety, mechanism, number of patients, and need for additional resources
Skipping PPE to save time
Moving unstable patients before stabilizing life threats

explanation: Standard scene approach emphasizes safety, BSI, MOI, and resource requests.

distractorExplanations.a: Demographics come after safety and assessment.

prompt: You find an unresponsive adult without pulse. After activating EMS and getting an AED, you should:

options.text: Wait for the AED before any compressions
Deliver breaths only without compressions
Start chest compressions at 100–120/min allowing full recoil
Check blood glucose for 5 minutes first

explanation: BLS for adult cardiac arrest emphasizes immediate high-quality compressions and early AED.

distractorExplanations.a: Compressions start while AED is retrieved unless unsafe.
distractorExplanations.b: Compressions are primary for witnessed adult arrest.
distractorExplanations.d: Do not delay CPR for routine glucose check.

prompt: FAST stroke assessment includes checking for:

options.text: Fever, asthma, seizures, and trauma only
Foot pain and ankle swelling
Blood type and allergies only
Facial droop, arm drift, speech difficulty, and time of onset

explanation: Face, Arms, Speech, Time helps identify stroke and urgency for transport.

distractorExplanations.a: Not FAST components.

prompt: A diabetic patient is confused, diaphoretic, and tremulous with a glucose of 52 mg/dL. You should:

options.text: Administer oral glucose if awake and able to swallow safely
Withhold all sugar even if swallowing is intact
Give only high-dose insulin
Place in Trendelenburg for head injury

explanation: Conscious hypoglycemia with intact swallow gets fast-acting oral glucose per protocol.

distractorExplanations.b: Oral glucose is first line when safe to swallow.

prompt: Anaphylaxis with wheeze and hypotension after bee sting requires:

options.text: Antihistamine alone without transport
Epinephrine auto-injector per protocol and rapid transport
Waiting to see if hives fade
Cold packs only on the sting site

explanation: Anaphylaxis is treated with IM epinephrine and urgent ALS/transport.

distractorExplanations.a: Antihistamines are adjuncts, not sole therapy.
distractorExplanations.d: Local care does not treat systemic anaphylaxis.

prompt: A patient with crushing substernal chest pain and nitroglycerin prescribed should receive NTG only if:

options.text: Systolic BP is adequate per protocol and patient has not taken PDE-5 inhibitor recently
Patient took sildenafil within 24–48 hours per drug interaction rules
Patient is hypotensive below 90 systolic
Pain is clearly from ankle sprain

explanation: Nitroglycerin is contraindicated in hypotension and recent PDE-5 inhibitor use.

distractorExplanations.a: Adequate BP and no recent PDE-5 use are required.
distractorExplanations.d: Chest pain protocol applies to suspected cardiac pain.

prompt: Syncope with rapid recovery in a hot environment suggests:

options.text: Immediate surgical airway
Mandatory tourniquet application
No assessment needed if patient stands up
Heat-related illness; move to cool area and assess hydration/perfusion

explanation: Heat exhaustion/syncope needs cooling, rest, and assessment for worsening heat stroke.

distractorExplanations.a: Airway is not first unless compromised.

prompt: Seizure activity has stopped and the patient is postictal but breathing adequately. You should:

options.text: Place in recovery position, protect from injury, monitor airway, and time transport
Restrain all four limbs tightly during postictal state
Place objects in the mouth to prevent tongue biting
Withhold oxygen despite hypoxia

explanation: Post-seizure care focuses on airway, protection, oxygen if needed, and evaluation.

distractorExplanations.b: Restraint can cause injury; protect environment instead.
distractorExplanations.c: Nothing goes in the mouth during seizures.

prompt: Which vital sign pattern suggests decompensated shock in a medical patient?

options.text: HR 72, BP 120/80, warm skin, alert
Heart rate 130, BP 86/60, cool skin, altered mental status
RR 12 calm with normal mentation only
Temp 98.6°F with no other findings

explanation: Tachycardia, hypotension, poor perfusion, and AMS suggest shock.

distractorExplanations.a: Normal vitals argue against shock.
distractorExplanations.d: Normal temp alone does not rule out shock.

prompt: A patient with asthma has silent chest and exhaustion. This indicates:

options.text: Improving asthma ready for discharge
Need for only antihistamine PO
Impending respiratory failure requiring urgent transport and advanced care
No oxygen because CO2 retention never occurs

explanation: Silent chest and fatigue are ominous signs of severe bronchospasm/respiratory failure.

distractorExplanations.a: Silent chest is dangerous.
distractorExplanations.d: Severe asthma may need controlled O2 and ventilatory support.

prompt: For suspected opioid overdose with respiratory depression, EMR care includes:

options.text: Stimulating only without ventilatory support
High-dose opioid to prevent withdrawal
Delayed transport until patient sleeps
Airway/ventilation support, naloxone per protocol, and transport

explanation: Opioid overdose management prioritizes ventilation and naloxone when authorized.

distractorExplanations.a: Ventilation is critical if respirations are inadequate.
distractorExplanations.c: Somnolence with depressed respirations is emergent.

prompt: Chest pain patient with aspirin allergy and no contraindications otherwise: aspirin should be:

options.text: Given if protocol allows after allergy assessment
Mandatory regardless of anaphylaxis history
Replaced with high-dose insulin
Withheld and patient left without transport

explanation: True aspirin allergy contraindicates; otherwise chewable aspirin may be given per protocol.

distractorExplanations.c: Insulin is not chest pain treatment.

prompt: A febrile patient with altered mental status and hot dry skin may have:

options.text: Simple viral cold only
Heat stroke requiring rapid cooling and transport
Hypothermia
Normal variation requiring no action

explanation: Heat stroke presents with CNS dysfunction and hot skin; it is life-threatening.

distractorExplanations.a: Altered mental status suggests severity beyond cold.

prompt: When assisting a prescribed epinephrine auto-injector, the injection site is typically:

options.text: Over the heart
Into a vein only
Outer mid-thigh (vastus lateralis)
Into the buttock subcutaneously always

explanation: Auto-injectors are administered IM in the anterolateral thigh.

distractorExplanations.a: Not an appropriate IM site for epinephrine.
distractorExplanations.d: Thigh is preferred over buttock for absorption.

prompt: Priority for a patient with severe abdominal pain and rigid abdomen includes:

options.text: Large meal to test tolerance
Aggressive oral hydration despite vomiting
Delay transport for home remedies
Keep NPO, monitor vitals, position of comfort, rapid transport

explanation: Acute rigid abdomen may indicate surgical emergency; NPO and urgent transport are key.

distractorExplanations.a: NPO is standard.
distractorExplanations.b: Oral intake may worsen some conditions.

prompt: Pulse oximetry reading 88% on room air in a symptomatic patient should prompt:

options.text: Oxygen per protocol and reassessment of work of breathing
Ignore because oximetry is always wrong
Discharge without transport
Only document without intervention

explanation: Hypoxemia with symptoms warrants supplemental oxygen and further assessment/transport.

distractorExplanations.b: Low reading with symptoms needs action.

prompt: Upon arrival at a motor vehicle collision, the EMR's first priority is:

options.text: Patient care before any scene safety assessment
Determine scene safety and use appropriate PPE
Interview witnesses for legal statements only
Begin triage without notifying dispatch of hazards

explanation: Scene safety and BSI precede patient contact per standard EMS operations.

distractorExplanations.a: Unsafe scenes endanger responders and patients.
distractorExplanations.c: Witnesses are secondary to safety and care.

prompt: A competent adult refuses transport after assessment. The EMR should:

options.text: Force transport against will
Destroy all documentation to avoid liability
Explain risks, ensure understanding, document refusal, and leave contact/return instructions per protocol
Threaten arrest for refusal

explanation: Informed refusal requires capacity assessment, risk explanation, documentation, and medical control if needed.

distractorExplanations.a: Forcing transport violates autonomy absent exigent circumstances.

prompt: Protected health information (PHI) on a PCR may be shared with:

options.text: Anyone on social media for education
Media without consent
Curious bystanders for updates
Receiving facility and authorized personnel involved in patient care

explanation: HIPAA permits disclosure for treatment, payment, and healthcare operations on need-to-know basis.

distractorExplanations.a: Social media disclosure violates privacy.
distractorExplanations.c: Bystanders are not entitled to PHI.

prompt: Standard precautions require:

options.text: Gloves and hand hygiene at minimum when contact with blood/body fluids is possible
No PPE if patient looks clean
Reuse gloves between patients without cleaning
Only airborne precautions for all calls

explanation: Standard precautions treat all blood/body fluids as potentially infectious.

distractorExplanations.d: Standard precautions apply broadly; airborne is specific.

prompt: When requesting additional resources, the EMR should provide:

options.text: Only patient favorite color
Location, nature of incident, hazards, number of patients, and resources needed
Personal opinions about fault
Delayed notification until all care finished without updates

explanation: Clear concise radio/reporting includes location, problem, hazards, and resource needs.

distractorExplanations.a: Irrelevant to resource allocation.
distractorExplanations.c: Fault determination is not dispatch priority.

prompt: Medical direction may be required for:

options.text: Every bandage application universally
Opening the ambulance door
Certain medications, protocols outside standing orders, or complex refusals per local policy
Hand washing

explanation: Online/offline medical control governs extensions beyond standing orders and some refusals.

distractorExplanations.a: Basic first aid often under standing orders.
distractorExplanations.d: Infection control standard, not MD order.

prompt: At a hazardous materials incident, the EMR should:

options.text: Enter the hot zone to retrieve victims without training
Mix unknown chemicals to neutralize them
Ignore wind direction
Recognize hazard, protect self, deny entry, and request hazmat specialists

explanation: Hazmat scenes require recognition, isolation, and specialist response beyond EMR scope.

distractorExplanations.a: Untrained entry increases casualties.

prompt: Documentation on the PCR should be:

options.text: Objective, timely, and include assessment findings, interventions, and patient responses
Filled with abbreviations only you understand years later
Altered after legal review to look better
Omitted when patient refuses care

explanation: Accurate objective documentation supports continuity of care and legal record.

prompt: During mass casualty triage, a patient with absent respirations after airway opening may be classified:

options.text: Immediate (red) always regardless of resources
Expectant/black in many triage systems when resources are limited and no spontaneous respirations after opening airway
Minor/green
Delayed/yellow solely for ankle sprain

explanation: START and similar systems tag non-breathing after airway maneuver as expectant when resources constrained.

distractorExplanations.a: Without respirations after maneuver, immediate lifesaving may be futile at MCI.
distractorExplanations.d: Ankle sprain alone is not the scenario.

prompt: Consent for treatment of an unconscious adult is generally:

options.text: Never possible
Requires written contract signed while unconscious
Implied consent for emergency care
Only from police at scene

explanation: Implied consent allows emergency treatment when patient cannot consent and delay would harm.

distractorExplanations.a: Emergency exceptions exist.
distractorExplanations.d: Police do not substitute for medical consent rules.

prompt: An EMR discovers possible child abuse during a call. Appropriate action includes:

options.text: Ignore unless parent admits guilt
Confront alleged abuser violently
Delete findings from PCR
Report per local mandatory reporting laws and ensure child safety

explanation: Mandatory reporters must notify appropriate authorities and document objective findings.

distractorExplanations.a: Suspicion triggers reporting duty.

prompt: Vehicle staging at a violent scene should be:

options.text: Upwind/uphill at a safe distance until law enforcement secures scene when indicated
Inside the active shooter hot zone
Blocking all egress for other units unnecessarily
With lights/sirens off only after leaving jurisdiction

explanation: Staging protects crews until scene is safe or escorted by law enforcement.

distractorExplanations.b: Hot zones are unsafe without tactical coordination.
distractorExplanations.c: Do not impede other emergency vehicles.

prompt: After patient transfer to ALS, the EMR should:

options.text: Leave without communication
Provide concise verbal report and complete PCR including times and interventions
Withhold allergy information
Discard all equipment without restocking documentation

explanation: Handoff communication and complete documentation ensure continuity of care.

distractorExplanations.a: Report is essential.

prompt: Which action violates ethical boundaries?

options.text: Maintaining professional demeanor
Respecting cultural preferences when safe
Accepting a large cash gift from a patient for preferential future response
Declining to work while impaired

explanation: Accepting gifts for preferential treatment creates conflict of interest and unethical conduct.

distractorExplanations.a: Professionalism is expected.
distractorExplanations.b: Cultural respect within safety limits is appropriate.

prompt: When terminating resuscitation in the field is considered (per protocol/medical direction), factors include:

options.text: Responder fatigue alone without protocol
Patient shoe brand
Bystander request only without medical criteria
Downtime without ROSC, unsafe scene, valid DNR, and medical direction criteria

explanation: Termination follows protocol/medical direction considering downtime, DNR, scene safety, and futility criteria.

distractorExplanations.a: Fatigue alone is insufficient.
